title: Agent spec and agent instance are orthogonal layers, not competing identity schemes
content: Agent spec and agent instance are orthogonal layers, not competing identity schemes. The spec is the immutable recipe — typically a JCS-SHA over {system_prompt, tools, model, ...} — and persists across runs. The instance is the ephemeral execution: a process, a Worker invocation, a container, whatever runs the inference loop. Spec answers "what is this agent." Instance answers "which copy of it is acting right now." Both have IDs, both matter, neither replaces the other. Identity-as-spec gets you reproducibility, audit, and equivalence checking. Identity-as-instance gets you provenance and lifecycle. Earlier framing in AMS.md treated these as competing options; that was wrong and has been corrected.

title: AMS does not own the deterministic harness pattern
content: AMS does not own the deterministic harness pattern. The harness is a subscriber pattern that emerges once AMS exists — anyone can build it, including us, but it is not a primitive the protocol owns. The vodka contract holds: AMS carries tokens, full stop. Spec format, spec verification, instance lifecycle, and orchestration choreography are all harness-level concerns. AMS provides the wire that makes the pattern possible.

title: Patterns built on AMS are documented in PATTERNS.md
content: Patterns built on AMS are documented in PATTERNS.md, not in PROTOCOL.md or AMS.md. PROTOCOL.md is the wire spec. AMS.md is the conceptual thesis. PATTERNS.md is the catalog of "things you can build because AMS exists." This separation keeps the protocol minimal and gives emergent patterns a place to grow without polluting the spec.

title: The deterministic harness pattern collapses orchestration into a subscriber role
content: The deterministic harness pattern collapses orchestration into a subscriber role. Today, agent orchestration is bolted into individual runtimes (Claude Code's orchestration layer, Bot Framework's orchestration layer, etc.) and every framework owns its own. Once AMS exists, "be the orchestrator" reduces to "subscribe to a magic link and decide what to spawn." Anyone can be the orchestrator because anyone can be a subscriber. This is a structural unlock — not a feature AMS provides, but a category of work that the foundation makes radically simpler.

title: AMS as currently scoped is an intranet for agents, not the internet for agents
content: AMS as currently scoped is an intranet for agents, not the internet for agents. The single-hosted-instance model lets agents within reach of that instance talk; cross-instance communication requires a federation protocol layer that does not yet exist. The intranet/internet distinction is honest and worth keeping in the framing — federation is the thing standing between AMS-as-it-is and AMS-as-internet.

title: Spec canonicalization scope is unresolved
content: Spec canonicalization scope is unresolved. Minimum is system_prompt + tools + model. Whether runtime parameters (temperature, top-p, seed) belong in the canonical bootstrap is undecided. Same shape of problem as JCS-SHA conversation identifiers; probably worth a single canonicalization pass that covers both.

title: Spec distribution is undecided
content: Spec distribution is undecided. The harness needs the spec to spawn the instance. Three plausible designs: inlined in conversation metadata, fetched from a known spec store, or carried as the first token in the conversation. Likely a harness-level choice, not an AMS-level decision, but worth explicit acknowledgement.

title: Instance lifecycle authority is undecided
content: Instance lifecycle authority is undecided. Who decides when an instance terminates — the harness on a timeout, the orchestrator via a kill token, the instance itself, or all three depending on deployment? Likely all three; needs to be a per-deployment configuration rather than a baked decision.

title: Intranet for agents (current scope) vs Internet for agents (federation goal)
content: Intranet for agents (current scope) vs Internet for agents (federation goal). AMS today provides intranet semantics: a single hosted instance, conversations within that instance, no cross-instance routing. The internet-for-agents framing requires federation, which is an open protocol layer above AMS. The walk-back from "internet" to "intranet" in the brain dump is correct and should be preserved in positioning until federation lands.
